Based on Management representation, we understand Target engages individuals that are tax resident in countries outside of Canada, including India and various countries in Europe. We understand these individuals are contracted by Target and all agreed to sign an independent contractor agreement. However, Management represented that the contractors are in effect treated as employees of Target, as such persons have been working exclusively with Target as software developers and customer service representatives.

As the employment is exercised outside of Canada, Canadian payroll withholding taxes should not apply. However, based on Management’s representations, there is a medium to high risk that the relevant tax authority in the respective foreign jurisdictions may classify the independent contractors as employees of Target. Consequently, local payroll withholdings and other source deductions may need to be withheld and remitted to such tax authority. Target may also be exposed to penalties and interest on unremitted employee source deductions and certain social security taxes in the respective foreign jurisdictions. Further, subject to specific rules in the relevant jurisdictions, there may be secondary liability for Target or its directors for the principal portion of any unremitted taxes.

Based on review of a subcontractor listing provided in the VDR, and on the assumption that foreign employer payroll obligations are similar to Canadian payroll obligations, for illustrative purposes, we have estimated a potential cash tax exposure of $300,000-400,000.

As non-Canadian tax due diligence is outside of our scope, we recommend obtaining a full indemnity for any historical tax liabilities that could arise as a result of Target engaging contractors that may be more appropriately classified as employees in other jurisdictions.

Management and Tax Advisor represented that 90%-95% of Target’s total sales were to US-based customers, and Target engages 5 US-based individuals on a full-time basis. Management represented Target pays these individuals through a professional employment organization that collects and pay taxes for each employee based on the states where they live. Tax Advisor clarified that these individuals are not on the payroll of Axiom.

A company may create a PE through the activities of a person acting on the company’s behalf in a foreign country.  A PE generally exists if employees or other dependent agents in the source country have authority to conclude sales contracts in the company’s name. If a taxpayer has a PE in a foreign country, it could be subject to filing requirements and incur tax liabilities. Management represented that two of the US-based individuals act as relationship personnel to US-based clients, and these individuals do not maintain the right to conclude contracts and all contracts are negotiated and executed in Canada. Management represented the other three individuals do not deliver any services in the US as well. As a result, Management represented Target has no permanent establishment in the US.

On this basis that Management and Tax Advisor represented that Target has no US employees, no contracts are ever signed or approved in the US, and no services are delivered in the US or to US customers, and no US permanent establishment exists for Target, Target has never registered or filed any income or indirect US tax compliance filings. It appears reasonable to conclude that no US filings are warranted. Although there is a low risk of a potential tax liability or cash tax exposure in the US, we recommend that Axiom file treaty exempt returns for historical periods and going-forward.

We performed a high level impact assessment of the adoption of ASC 606 by Axiom. Our observations are based on the readily available materials, such as the audited financial statements, sample contracts, interview with management and other materials that were provided by Axiom. Our impact assessment indicates areas that are likely to be impacted by ASC 606 for revenue recognition upon adoption of the standard. It is noted that additional assessment considerations could be identified upon implementation of the new revenue recognition standard. In our experience, in the implementation phase more details and facts and circumstances are typically identified, because a larger sample of contracts will need to be reviewed and more corroborative inquiry (for example with operations personnel) may lead to new insights and newly identified impact areas. Below the main impacted areas that were identified in the impact assessment are included.

Axiom recognizes revenue based on specific activities that are bucketed into two categories ‘Think/Deploy’ and ‘Launch’. Under ASC 606, this current split of revenues may not be aligned with the identification of performance obligations, which will affect both the timing and amount of revenue recognized. Particularly, if some of the activities related to ‘Think/Deploy’ are determined to be set-up activities (e.g. activation activities or set-up of user interfaces), fees related to these activities will need to be deferred and recognized as part of other performance obligations.

For each performance obligation identified, the stand-alone selling price will need to be determined. Management represented that the stated contract prices are considered to be an appropriate basis to determine stand-alone selling prices. However, evidence to support this statement likely may require implementing a new process. In addition, the level (i.e. the grouping of activities) at which stand-alone selling price is estimated is directly affected by the determination of performance obligations discussed above.

We note that the above potential impact areas are judgemental. Depending on the application of judgement and evaluation of the facts and circumstances of individual contracts, it is possible that the Company may arrive at similar revenue recognition to their current accounting. Conversely, if changes to the accounting are required, the amount of the impact likely would vary from one contract to another and therefore is very difficult to accurately estimate or extrapolate. These areas of judgement have been discussed in more detail on the following pages. A high level quantification of the potential impact, including the related assumptions, is included on the next page.

On the earlier of the signing of the share purchase agreement or binding letter of intent, where a buyer (i.e., either a non-resident corporation or a Canadian corporation, that is controlled by a non-resident company) obtains the right to acquire more than 50% of the shares of a Canadian target, the Canadian target may lose its CCPC status.

That is, on the acquisition of the majority of the issued and outstanding shares of the Company, Buyer should have the right to control the Company, such that the Company will be controlled directly or indirectly in any manner whatever by a non-resident. As a result the Company is expected to lose its CCPC status.

Axiom will have a deemed taxation year-end immediately before the time of losing its CCPC status. Axiom will be required to file a corporate tax return for the deemed year-end, due six months following the deemed year-end.
We generally recommend, where otherwise feasible, the SPA is signed on the day of closing to limit the number of deemed year ends.
Where the SPA is signed in advance of closing, Axiom will have two deemed year-ends, that is (i) on the loss of CCPC status and (ii) on the AOC.

When the shares of Target are acquired, there will be an acquisition of control (“AOC”) of Axiom for Canadian income tax purposes. The AOC rules will generally result in a deemed taxation year-end for Axiom. Corporate income tax returns for the deemed year-end will be due within 6 months after the deemed year-end.  Final tax payments will be due 2 months after the deemed year-end.

The AOC rules will generally result in the realization of any inherent losses on certain assets, as well as a write-down of assets to their FMV.  Furthermore, existing NOLs will be restricted post-acquisition; and any previously unutilized capital or property losses will expire.
Under the AOC rules, the use of any NOLs of Target that may arise in FY19 or pre-AOC could be restricted as follows:
The NOLs can only be utilized if the business that incurred the NOLs is carried on for profit throughout the particular year in which the carry forwards are utilized; and

The NOLs can only be applied against income from the business that incurred the carry forwards and from any other business substantially all of the income of which was derived from the sale of similar properties or similar services.
As of FY18, Target has utilized all of its non-capital loss carry forwards.

text: The objective of this step is to allocate the transaction price to each performance obligation in an amount that depicts the amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled

section: Profit and Loss Overview / Financial Performance
text: for transferring the promised goods or service to the customer. Generally, an entity accomplishes this objective by allocating the transaction price to each performance obligation on a relative stand-alone selling price (SSP) basis.

section: Profit and Loss Overview / Financial Performance
text: The best evidence of a performance obligation’s SSP is an observable price from stand-alone sales. A contractually stated contract price or list price could be the SSP, but this is not presumed to be the case and would need to be supported by other evidence. If there is no observable SSP available, the entity is required to determine an estimated SSP.

section: Profit and Loss Overview / Financial Performance
text: Despite the abovementioned, an entity shall allocate a variable amount entirely to a performance obligation (or a distinct good or service that forms part of a single performance obligation) if the terms of the variable payment relate specifically to that performance obligation and if this allocation method is consistent with the overall allocation objective of ASC 606.

text: Contracts contain detailed overviews of all the activities to be performed in each phase. This overview also indicates the unit price of each item and the extended price that will be charged to the customer. Per discussion with Management, the amounts for each activity are generally based on the market rates – pricing for projects is consistent, subject to adjustments to reflect the requirements (e.g. number of patients, number of sites, complexity). Axiom may provide enterprise discounts to its customers, but such a discount percentage is typically provided over all of the goods and services. Because of this pricing structure, the allocation objective of ASC 606 would be met if all of the activities correspond to performance obligations.

text: Management noted that providing various discounts on certain promises in the contract, as opposed to the entire contract, could happen occasionally. This may relate to adjustments to reflect the circumstances of the particular contract (e.g. complexity of that element), in which case the adjusted price may reflect the SSP for that particular project. However, if significant specific discounts were offered as an incentive or without a particular rationale, an allocation of the discount to between the performance obligations in the contract would be applicable. Given that these types of adjustments occur rarely, the impact is not expected to be material.

text: The transaction price allocated to a performance obligation is recognized when (or as) the performance obligation is satisfied,

section: Profit and Loss Overview / Financial Performance
text: i.e. when (or as) the customers obtains control of the promised goods or services.

section: Profit and Loss Overview / Financial Performance
text: Revenue is recognized over time if one of the three criteria for over time recognition is met: (1) the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the entity’s performance as the entity performs, (2) the entity’s performance creates or enhances an asset that the customer controls as the asset is created or enhanced, or (3) the entity’s performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the entity, and the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date. If none of these criteria is met, the revenues are recognized at the point in time at which control of the good or service transfers to the customer.

section: Profit and Loss Overview / Financial Performance
text: When an entity charges a consistent per transaction or per usage fee, the variable fees can be allocated entirely to the period in which they are earned if the criteria of the variable consideration allocation guidance (refer to step 4) are met. This is because the changes amounts reflect changes in the value to the customer, i.e. the value to the customer of its access to the managed services correlates with how much the customer makes use of the managed services.

Risk of ITC reduction under a detailed CRA review

A detailed review can be either a technical or financial review and typically 20% of all SR&ED claims submitted to CRA annually are selected for detailed review. Target’s FY17 SR&ED claim has been selected for detailed review by CRA.

A detailed technical review would involve a review of the science behind the project being claimed. During this type of review, CRA will generally review both the technical eligibility of the submission and the eligibility of the associated costs. In the event of a detailed technical review, there is a risk that significant portions of the claim could be denied because under a detailed technical review the entire project submitted could be disallowed.

A detailed financial review only generally involves a review of the appropriateness of the associated costs. In the event of a detailed financial review, the risk of a reduction to the ITCs claimed is generally much lower than a detailed technical review as only the ineligible expenditures of a project are disallowed.

Over the past few years we have noted that CRA has become increasingly stringent during technical reviews and has adopted a narrower view of the types of activities that qualify for SR&ED tax credits.

Canada has a SR&ED tax incentive regime which provides tax benefits to corporations carrying out qualifying activities (i.e., “systematic investigation or research carried out in a field of science or technology by means of experiment, analysis or modification”).

Federal ITCs are available for eligible CCPCs at a refundable enhanced rate of 35% on (up to C$3 million) of eligible expenditures versus a non-refundable rate of 15% that is available to non-CCPCs (and CCPCs with eligible expenditures above C$3 million). ITCs are included in taxable income in the year following the year in which the ITC was received.
The expenditure limit for refundable Federal ITCs at the reduced rate may be reduced where a corporation’s aggregate amount of taxable capital and taxable income exceed certain thresholds.

Provincial OITCs are available for all corporations with a permanent establishment (“PE”) in Ontario at a refundable rate of 8% on up to C$3 million of eligible expenditures. OITCs are included in taxable income in the year following the year received.
Similar to federal incentives, the expenditure limit may be reduced where a corporation’s aggregate amount of taxable capital and taxable income exceed certain thresholds.
ii. Ontario research and development tax credit (“ORDTC”)
Provincial ORDTCs are available for all Canadian corporations with a PE in Ontario at a non-refundable rate of 3.5% of eligible expenditures.

The SR&ED eligible work activities for which the ITCs are granted have to meet a strict CRA criteria, known as a 5 part eligibility test. Based on the content of project descriptions (form T661, Part 2) for fiscal FY16

two SR&ED projects claimed do not pass the eligibility test. More specifically, a) there does not appear to be evidence of identification of scientific or technological uncertainties (TUs). The TUs mentioned in the report do not appear to be technological in nature and there is little evidence of due-diligence activities performed to confirm existing knowledge, methods, tools, frameworks etc. could not be used to overcome the TUs; b) the work performed generally does not support ‘systematic investigation or search’ and appears to be routine engineering/development, using known methods, tools, knowledge etc.; and c) the technological advancements (TAs) mentioned are do not appear technological in nature; they do not convey an advancement in the underlying area of science or technology.

contractors status is question of fact for Canadian tax purposes. Factors considered by the CRA in order to determine if any individual is an independent contractor or employee include: degree of control over the worker, tool/equipment used by the worker, subcontracting work by the worker, financial risk taken by the worker, etc.

Where an individual independent contractor is determined to be factually an employee, the employer may be liable for amounts on remuneration not withheld and remitted, including benefits such as CPP, EI, and payroll taxes to the extent that they were under or unpaid by the employee/contractor, in addition to penalties and interest related to its failure to withhold and remit these amounts.
